title: UTC Technical Note
subtitle: Draft note on compatibility, symbol identity, spatial composition, and next steps
summary: A formal draft note stating that official Unicode SignWriting can name much of the character set but still does not preserve stable symbol identity or provide compatible written-sign support.
synopsis: This draft UTC note states the current technical position in a formal register. It separates character naming from stable symbol identity and written-sign encoding, explains how the facial-diacritic model shifts part of symbol formation into font behavior, identifies spatial composition and collation as unresolved issues, and requests renewed technical review rather than automatic migration pressure.

slides.title: Current UTC-Facing Position
slides.summary: The current official Unicode SignWriting encoding is not sufficient for compatible production use within the Sutton SignWriting ecosystem.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for UTC technical note baseline.
bullets: Compatibility is the core issue
The gap is not only historical or rhetorical
It is technical and production-based
The current ecosystem is the standard of judgment
slides.speech: The UTC-facing position should be stated plainly. The current official Unicode SignWriting encoding is not sufficient for compatible production use within the Sutton SignWriting ecosystem. The issue is technical and production-based. The relevant standard is not theoretical possibility. The relevant standard is whether the model can carry the real ecosystem without loss, redesign, or incompatible workarounds.

slides.title: Three Different Questions
slides.summary: Naming characters, preserving stable writer-selected symbols, and encoding complete written signs are different questions.
bullets: The official model can name much of the character set
That is not the same as preserving stable writer-selected symbols
That is not the same as encoding complete written signs
The current official model reaches the first point much more than the second or third
slides.speech: The core distinction should be stated directly. Naming characters is one question. Preserving stable writer-selected symbols is another. Encoding written signs is a third. The current official model can name much of the character set. That does not mean it preserves stable symbol identity across the full system, and it does not mean it provides a compatible written-sign model. Once these three questions are separated, the present compatibility critique becomes clearer and more precise.
slides.notes: This replaces the older two-layer wording with the clearer three-part distinction.

slides.title: Character Naming Is Not Enough
slides.summary: The official block covers base, fill, and rotation characters, but that does not settle symbol identity or written-sign support.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for character naming not being enough.
bullets: The official block covers base characters, fills, and rotations
That does not guarantee stable symbol identity across the full system
That does not provide a closed shared facial inventory
That does not provide a compatible written-sign model
slides.speech: The official block does cover base characters, fills, and rotations. That matters. But it is not enough by itself. Character naming does not guarantee stable symbol identity across the full system, it does not provide a closed shared facial inventory, and it does not provide a compatible model for written signs. This is the point where a symbol block and a usable writing system part company.
slides.notes: This slide gives the explicit technical bridge from characters to the deeper issues.

slides.title: The Facial System Moves Symbol Formation Into The Font
slides.summary: The facial-diacritic model delegates part of symbol formation to font behavior rather than keeping it fixed in a writer-selected inventory.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the facial-diacritic model.
bullets: Formal SignWriting expects writer-selected ISWA symbols
The official facial model writes a sequence merged by the font
There is no closed shared master inventory of final combinations
Font behavior becomes part of symbol determination
slides.speech: The facial system deserves special attention because it is not just a rendering variation. In Formal SignWriting, the writer selects a symbol from the shared inventory. In the official facial-diacritic model, the writer writes a sequence that the font merges into a rendered face. That moves part of symbol determination into font behavior. Without a closed shared master inventory of final combinations, different implementations are pushed toward copying one interpretation or inventing another.
slides.notes: This is the most technically pointed slide. Keep it calm.

slides.title: Collation Is A Signal Of Deeper Incompatibility
slides.summary: Sorting is not a minor tuning issue. It reveals whether the model aligns with practical symbol identity and workflow needs.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for sorting and collation.
bullets: Dictionaries and corpora depend on stable ordering
Facial ambiguity already weakens stable symbol identity
Inherent first fill and first rotation weaken explicit identity
Collation problems indicate a deeper model mismatch
Restoring explicit F1 and R1 would move back toward compatibility
slides.speech: Collation should not be treated as a small follow-up detail. Dictionaries, corpora, search, and workflow all depend on stable ordering. In the current official model, the facial system already weakens stable symbol identity, and inherent first fill and first rotation values further complicate explicit ordering. This is why collation is best treated as a signal of deeper model mismatch. A cleaner path toward compatibility would restore explicit first fill and first rotation values rather than treating them as inherent.
slides.notes: This is where the F1/R1 point belongs.

slides.title: The Ecosystem Cannot Be Asked To Migrate Blindly
slides.summary: A standards path becomes valuable when it can serve as stable interchange for real production use. That test has not yet been met.
bullets: The ecosystem already includes real software, data, and publications
A migration path must preserve stable symbols and written signs across those assets
Blind migration pressure is not technical progress
Compatibility must be demonstrated, not presumed
slides.speech: The Sutton SignWriting ecosystem already includes real software, real datasets, real corpora, and real publications. A standards path becomes valuable when it can serve as stable interchange for that kind of production use. That means preserving stable symbols and written signs across the existing assets, not only naming characters differently. Without a demonstrated compatibility path, migration pressure is not technical progress. Compatibility has to be shown, not assumed.
slides.notes: This slide grounds the standards argument in the existing ecosystem.

slides.title: Requested Next Step
slides.summary: The immediate request is modest: accurate acknowledgment of the present gap and a supported path for renewed technical review.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the requested next step.
bullets: Acknowledge current incompatibility
Acknowledge that stable symbol identity is not preserved cleanly
Acknowledge unresolved spatial composition
Acknowledge that part of facial symbol formation is font-mediated
Review symbol-model and collation issues honestly
Open a supported technical follow-up path
slides.speech: The requested next step is modest and concrete. Acknowledge that the current official model is not sufficient for compatible production use. Acknowledge that stable symbol identity is not preserved cleanly, especially in the facial system where symbol formation is partly mediated through font behavior. Acknowledge that spatial composition remains unresolved. Review symbol-model and collation issues honestly. Then open a supported technical follow-up path. That is the disciplined starting point.
slides.notes: Close on the formal request, not the grievance.
